title: Japan business mood hits 8-year high, builds case for BOJ rate hikes
full_text:
By Leika Kihara

TOKYO, July 1 (Reuters) - Japanese business mood climbed to an eight-year high and corporate inflation expectations rose to record levels, a quarterly survey showed, underlining the economy's resilience to the Middle East war and bolstering ‚Äåthe case for more rate hikes.

The data will be among factors affecting the Bank of Japan's quarterly growth and ‚Äåinflation forecasts due next month, which will offer clues on how soon it could raise interest rates again after a hike to a 31-year high of 1% ‚Äãin June.

The headline index measuring big manufacturers' business sentiment improved to +22 in June from +17 in March, the BOJ's "tankan" survey showed on Wednesday, beating market forecasts of +16 and marking the highest level since March 2018.

While rising costs and supply disruptions caused by the Iran war weighed on sentiment, the negative impact was offset by brisk demand for AI-related goods and chips, the data showed.

An index measuring big non-manufacturers' sentiment rose ‚Äåto +37 from +36 in March. It also topped ‚ÅÝmarket forecasts for +35 and hit the highest level since August 1991, on robust inbound tourism and progress firms made in passing on costs.

Companies expect inflation to hit 2.6% both three and five years from ‚ÅÝnow, up 10 basis points from the March survey and breaking record highs, the tankan showed, a sign firms see inflation staying well above the BOJ's 2% target for a prolonged period.

"It's a strong outcome that dispels concern the Middle East war could lead to a downturn in ‚ÄãJapan's ‚Äãeconomy," said Yoshiki Shinke, an economist at Dai-ichi Life Research Institute.

"What's also ‚Äãnotable is that corporate inflation expectations are creeping up, ‚Äåsuggesting mounting price pressures. All in all, the tankan builds the case for another rate hike by year-end, possibly in October," he said.

CLOUDS AHEAD

Big firms expect to increase capital expenditure by 11.5% in the current fiscal year ending in March 2027, compared with a median market forecast for a 10.5% gain, the survey showed.

Moderating the optimism, however, companies expect business conditions to worsen three months ahead as they brace for rising costs and potential supply constraints from the war, the survey showed.

The Middle East conflict has complicated the ‚ÄåBOJ's policy path, stoking inflation through higher oil prices while squeezing an ‚Äãeconomy dependent on imported fuel.

While the peace deal between the U.S. and Iran ‚Äãeased market fears over global price pressures, wholesale inflation spiked ‚Äãto a three-year high of 6.3% in May in a sign companies were already passing on higher ‚Äåcosts from the energy shock.
